intro:
Canaanite most often denotes the peoples who inhabited the land of Canaan before and alongside Israel. In the Table of Nations they descend from Canaan, son of Ham, whose line includes the Sidonians, Hittites, Jebusites, Amorites, Girgashites, Hivites, and others (Genesis 10:15–19) — the cluster commonly summarized as the "seven nations" Israel was to dispossess (Deuteronomy 7:1). In a narrower sense "Canaanite" names the traders and lowland dwellers of the region (the word also carried the sense "merchant"), a people of fortified city-states and advanced material culture whose worship of Baal, Asherah, and Molech — with its shrine prostitution and child sacrifice — Scripture presents as the depth of religious corruption (Leviticus 18:21–25).

The Canaanites stand at the heart of the land promise and its tensions. The territory was pledged to Abraham, yet its possession was deferred because "the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete" (Genesis 15:16) — a striking statement that the conquest, when it came, would be God's measured judgment on entrenched wickedness rather than mere conquest. Under Joshua, Israel was commanded to devote the Canaanite nations to destruction lest their idolatry corrupt the covenant people (Deuteronomy 20:16–18; Joshua 11:16–20). Yet Israel failed to drive them out fully (Judges 1:27–33); the remaining Canaanites became exactly the snare the law had warned of, drawing Israel into intermarriage and Baal worship throughout the era of the judges (Judges 2:11–13), and only under Solomon were the survivors reduced to forced labour (1 Kings 9:20–21).

Even so, the Canaanite is never beyond the reach of mercy. Rahab of Jericho, a Canaanite, hid the spies, confessed the LORD as God of heaven and earth, and was spared — taking her place in the ancestry of David and of Christ (Joshua 2:9–11; Matthew 1:5). In the Gospels a "Canaanite woman" of the region of Tyre and Sidon pressed her plea for her demon-tormented daughter and drew from Jesus the rare commendation, "O woman, great is your faith!" (Matthew 15:21–28) — a foretaste of the gospel reaching the nations.

The same word appears in a quite different sense as a personal epithet: one of the Twelve is called "Simon the Canaanite" (Matthew 10:4; Mark 3:18). Here the term is not ethnic but a transliteration of the Aramaic qan'ana, "zealot," rendered "Simon the Zealot" elsewhere (Luke 6:15; Acts 1:13) — marking either his former allegiance to the Zealot faction or his fervent temperament. Taken together, the Canaanites embody both a sober warning against the idolatry that destroys, and, in Rahab and the woman of faith, the surprising breadth of grace that gathers the nations to Israel's God.
